texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pressure swing adsorption (PSA) processes have been widely adopted in gas-treatment industries (Yang, 2013; Lively et al., 2009; Kärger and Ruthven, 1992), especially for upgrading unconventional gases, including coalbed methane (CBM) and landfill gas (LFG) (Xia et al., 2014; Aguilera et al., 2014). Membrane processes have also been recently implemented in commercial-scale gas-separation plants (Baker, 2002; Shi et al., 2016; Drioli and Romano, 2001; Strathmann, 2001; Esteves and Mota, 2007). These two processes have often been considered as alternatives to the conventional absorption and cryogenic processes (Esteves and Mota, 2007; Akinlabi et al., 2007; Nikoli´ c and Kikkinides, 2015), and they can be integrated into hybrid membrane/PSA processes (Akinlabi et al., 2007), which have advantages over standalone PSA or membrane processes in terms of plant size, product quality, and operating cost (Esteves and Mota, 2007).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The purpose of this conceptual design study is to evaluate coalbed methane (CBM) separation systems from an economic perspective. To achieve this goal, we propose several system configurations by combining membrane and pressure swing adsorption technologies with recycling. We then perform rigorous process simulations of the proposed CBM separation systems to meet the design specifications (CH4 purity of product stream: 97%, CH4 recovery rate: >90%) from a feed gas (CH4: 90% and CO2: 10%). We compare the systems using various technical and economic metrics, including methane recovery, process energy efficiency, and methane production cost. The top-performing system produces methane at a cost of 0.37 $/kg of CH4 (6.9 $/MMBTU), which can be further improved up to 0.27 $/kg of CH4 by applying promising scenarios.
texts.text: The purpose of this conceptual design study is to evaluate coalbed methane (CBM) separation systems from an economic perspective. To achieve this goal, we propose several system configurations by combining membrane and pressure swing adsorption technologies with recycling. We then perform rigorous process simulations of the proposed CBM separation systems to meet the design specifications (CH4 purity of product stream: 97%, CH4 recovery rate: >90%) from a feed gas (CH4: 90% and CO2: 10%). We compare the systems using various technical and economic metrics, including methane recovery, process energy efficiency, and methane production cost. The top-performing system produces methane at a cost of 0.37 $/kg of CH4 (6.9 $/MMBTU), which can be further improved up to 0.27 $/kg of CH4 by applying promising scenarios.

texts.orig: Membrane gas separation processes offer numerous benefits when used for CO2 capture and sequestration from gas mixtures (Akinlabi et al., 2007), including ease of operation, cost-effectiveness, and high energy efficiency (Grol, 2009). For these reasons, combining a membrane module with another separation process may further reduce costs and improve the separation efficiency (Nikoli´ c and Kikkinides, 2015; Bhide et al., 1998; Zhang et al., 2014; Zolandz and Fleming, 1992). For example, integrating a membrane module into a PSA process may increase the overall product recovery relative to PSA-only processes, which tend to have low product recoveries (Esteves and Mota, 2007; Akinlabi et al., 2007; Sircar and Golden, 2000). In addition, an integrated system could considerably improve the performance in terms of energy consumption and overall capital cost by reducing the size or number of adsorption beds (Nikoli´ c and Kikkinides, 2015).

Mercea and Hwang (1994) reported that the combination of a membrane process and an adsorption process affords superior economics and separation performance. Applying an integrated membrane/PSA process to fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) dry gas separation, Jiang et al. (1995) were able to recover most of the hydrogen in the membrane process and most of the ethylene and propylene in the PSA process. (2013) proposed an optimization strategy to maximize the performance of a three-stage gas (CH4/N2) separation plant composed of three types of units-membrane, PSA, and cryogenic separation processes-for CBM gas treatment. This optimization was conducted using a mixed integer nonlinear programming (MINLP) method within the given superstructure, where the material flow configuration was kept constant. Each stage included only one type of unit. First et al. developed a new optimization-based approach to evaluate economic performance of a wide variety of materials used in PSA technology for CH4/CO2 separation. As a result, they identified cost-effective adsorption materials along with operation conditions and discussed the correlation between the economical metrics and material-centric metrics, such as adsorption selectivity (First et al., 2014).

texts.orig: Generally, PSA is appropriate for obtaining high-purity product gases, while membrane processes are suitable for bulk separation (Feng et al., 1998). Thus, we can expect that hybridizing the two gas separation processes would offer synergistic effects. Nikoli´ c and Kikkinides proposed a modeling and optimization framework for hybrid PSA and membrane processes applied to a hydrogen production plant. They assessed their hydrogen recovery and separation performances as well as the downsizing of the adsorption bed offered by these processes. Recently, Warmuzinski et al. performed extensive experiments to investigate the separation capability of a hybrid system over a different CO2 concentration in flue gas. Substantial analysis along with a wealth of data were provided in their work, which are useful to R&D related to CO2 capture, in particular design and process integration and optimization of CO2 separation (Warmuzinski et al., 2015).

texts.orig: The simple hybrid process configurations can be classified into two categories: (1) membrane followed by PSA and (2) PSA followed by membrane. In the first category, bulk separation is mainly achieved by the membrane, and the purified product output by the membrane is then further purified by PSA to achieve a high-purity product. To increase the product recovery, the stream from the membrane to the PSA should have a low product concentration (Nikoli´ c and Kikkinides, 2015). In the second category, the membrane receives the waste gas from PSA to increase the recovery, and the adsorption beds can use the waste gas from the membrane for purging (Nikoli´ c and Kikkinides, 2015).

texts.orig: Esteves, 2005), little research has been conducted on the economics of various hybrid gas separation systems with different material flow configurations. In the present work, the CBM gas separation and purification performances of membrane and PSA processes are calculated using simulations conducted with gPROMS software, which was developed by Process Systems Enterprise, Ltd. On the basis of the predicted performance values, the economics of the hybrid systems are evaluated using the Aspen Process Economic Analyzer developed by Aspen Tech. We designed five candidate processes (hybrid membrane and PSA processes) comprising three stages, and some of the material flow configurations in the candidate hybrid systems differ. Each stage must include a PSA or membrane process. In the target hybrid systems, the adsorbent for the PSA process is zeolite 5A or a carbon molecular sieve (CMS), which adsorbs CO2 effectively. Thus, the economics of a total of ten candidate processes, i.e., five process configurations with two adsorbents, are analyzed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CBM, which is a natural gas adsorbed on coalbeds, is an economically important energy resource (James et al., 2008). CBM gas is mainly composed of methane, CO2 and minor components such as nitrogen (N2) and water. Thus, low-quality natural gas must be upgraded to satisfy the needs of downstream customers (Kidnay et al., 2011; GPSA, 2004; Khalilpour et al., 2013; Kohl and Nielsen, 1997; Daiminger and Lind, 2004).

texts.orig: Because this work is a conceptual design study of hybrid systems that can efficiently purify CBM gas, a simplified, twocomponent feed composition is considered. Note that while a typical raw CBM gas contains not only CH4 (89.5%) and CO2 (8.2%) but also water vapor (1.4%), N2 (0.9%) and other impurities, we simply assumed a mixture gas of CH4 (90%) and CO2 (10%) as the CBM feed stream (US Department of Energy, 2002). Theadopted feed flow rate is 936 N m 3 /h (or 247,600 MMBTU of CH4 per year) (US Department of Energy, 2002; Mu et al., 2015), whichcorresponds to an industrial-scale CBM extraction facility.

texts.orig: Thefour-bed, 12-operating-step PSA process studied in this work produces methane-rich gas (purified CBM gas) as well as CO2-rich gas, which serves as a waste or recycle stream. The adoptedmembranemodulefeaturesahollow-fibermembrane with counter-current flow. The bore sides within the fibers receive the feed flow and emit the retentate flow, which is the purified CBM gas. The permeate flow (CO2-rich gas) from the shell side is discharged as a waste or recycle stream.

To meet such high design specifications, multistage hybrid gas separation systems integrating PSA and membrane processes with a recycle stream are evaluated. For the multistage gas separation system, a simplified three-stage separation strategy is adopted in this study.

texts.orig: A large number of system configurations can be obtained by combining membrane and PSA technologies with a recycle stream. In our earlier study (Ko et al., 2013), we proposed various configurations to meet the design specifications (i.e., a certain level of purity and recovery of CH4) from different combinations of membrane and PSA technologies, and then briefly calculated the energy consumption of the major equipment. We here selected best out from the proposed systems on the basis of the energy consumption. The selected ten systems (the top-five configurations and two different operating conditions) are depicted in Fig. 1.

texts.orig: In Case 1, CBM gas is fed into the first membrane process after being pressurized to 10 bar through a multistage compression train (Stage 1). The permeate gas (1 bar, 25 ◦ C) from the first membrane process is merged with the recycle stream and then supplied to the second membrane process, which is operated at 1 bar and 25 ◦ C. The retentate gas (1 bar, 25 ◦ C) from the second membrane process enters the last separation process (PSA). Because the PSA subsystem uses zeolite 5A as an adsorbent, the adsorption pressure is relatively low (approximately 1bar at 25 ◦ C). While the purge stream of the PSA process is recycled after being compressed to 1bar, the purified stream and the retentate gas from the first membrane process are fed into a product storage tank.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Case 3, the pressurized CBM feed gas (10 bar) is merged with the recycle stream and then fed into the first membrane process, which is operated at 25 ◦ C. The permeate gas (1 bar, 25 ◦ C) from the first membrane process enters the second membrane process. The retentate gas from the second membrane module is compressed to 10bar and then delivered to the last separation process (PSA). While the exhaust gas of the PSA process is recycled and compressed to 10bar, the purified gas from the PSA process and the retentate gas from the first membrane process are transported to a product storage tank. Similar to the MrMP system, the PSA processes of Cases 3 and 4 use zeolite 5A and CMS, respectively. Thus, the adsorption pressures used in the PSA processes differ (1 bar for zeolite 5A and 10 bar for CMS), creating a difference in the utility consumptions of the two cases.
texts.text: In Case 3, the pressurized CBM feed gas (10 bar) is merged with the recycle stream and then fed into the first membrane process, which is operated at 25 ◦ C. The permeate gas (1 bar, 25 ◦ C) from the first membrane process enters the second membrane process. The retentate gas from the second membrane module is compressed to 10bar and then delivered to the last separation process (PSA). While the exhaust gas of the PSA process is recycled and compressed to 10bar, the purified gas from the PSA process and the retentate gas from the first membrane process are transported to a product storage tank. Similar to the MrMP system, the PSA processes of Cases 3 and 4 use zeolite 5A and CMS, respectively. Thus, the adsorption pressures used in the PSA processes differ (1 bar for zeolite 5A and 10 bar for CMS), creating a difference in the utility consumptions of the two cases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The configuration of the MMP system (Cases 5 and 6) sequentially comprises membrane, membrane, and PSA processes without any recycle, as described in Fig. 1c. The CBM feed gas is treated via a one-pass process to achieve the desired purity. An advantage of this system over the other systems is its smaller equipment size and lower utility consumption, as the flow rates of this system are lower than those of the systems with a recycle.

texts.text: 2.2.4. PrMM system

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this configuration, the CBM gas is treated by a PSA system and then by two membrane subsystems, as shown in Fig. 1d. The permeate gas from the second membrane module is recycled with the purge gas from the PSA system into the first membrane module. Because the first stage of the PrMM system is the PSA process, which purifies CBM gas more effectively than the membrane process, the remaining stages (two membrane subsystems) have lower operating loads than in the case of the hybrid system in which the first stage is the membrane module, reducing the flow rate of the inlet stream of both membrane systems. As in the previous cases, Cases 7 and 8 differ in the use of a zeolite 5A adsorbent (Case 7) or a CMS adsorbent (Case 8) in the PSA process.

texts.orig: The PrMP system (Cases 9 and 10) comprises two PSA processes and one membrane process with a recycle stream, as illustrated in Fig. 1e. The purge gas from the third stage (PSA) is merged with the purge gas from the first stage (PSA) and recycled into the second stage (membrane). This configuration is selected to analyze which technology best separates a relatively small flow feed stream. By comparison with the PrMM system, we can analyze the size and utility consumptions of the last subsystem (i.e., a membrane subsystem in PrMM vs. a PSA subsystem in PrMP). As in the previous systems, Cases 9 and 10 differ in the adsorbent used: in Case 9, CMS is used for the first-stage PSA and zeolite 5A for the third-stage PSA, while in Case 10, CMS is used for both PSA processes.

texts.orig: In the previous section, we introduced the candidate hybrid systems obtained by integrating PSA and membrane modules in various combinations with or without a recycle stream. The hybrid systems for the CBM gas upgrade were classified into membrane-based systems (MrMP, rMMP, and MMP) and PSAbasedsystems(PrMMandPrMP).Wealsoevaluatedtheimpact of a recycle step by comparing the rMMP system with the MMP system and evaluate the impact of the point at which the recy- cle stream is mixed with another stream by comparing the MrMP and rMMP systems. Finally, we assessed the effects of different PSA adsorbents (i.e., CMS and zeolite 5A) on the size and utility consumption of these systems. texts.orig: Next, we discuss whether the membrane process or PSA process is most appropriate for the first stage. Again, it should be noted that the first stage is the most important stage, as it processes the highest flow rate (i.e., the feed stream of the system) and accounts for the removal of the most methane from the product stream (approximately 84%). The CH4 recovery of the PrMP system, in which PSA is used in the first stage, is approximately 2% higher than that of the system in which the membrane process is first (MrMP). The superior separation performance of the PSA process leads to a higher CH4 recovery for the overall system.

Thus, this system has higher inlet flow rates for each stage and requires higher utility consumption (i.e., electricity and cooling water) for the pressure change. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Interestingly, Case 7 features the lowest utility consumption of all the systems. This is due to the constant pressure (1 bar) between the feed stream and the inlet stream of the first subsystem. Because the pressure required for the first stage of Case 7 (i.e., the zeolite-5A-packed PSA) is the same as that of the feed stream (1 bar), no pressurizing step is required for the feed stream. The elimination of this step reduces the total process energy consumption and results in Case 7 having the highest energy efficiency among the cases studied. Note that in the other cases, the pressurization of the feed stream accounts for approximately 80% of the total utility consumption.

texts.orig: We also analyze the effect of the choice of PSA adsorbent (CMS or zeolite 5A) on the utility consumptions. The cases adopting zeolite 5A as the PSA adsorbent tend to have lower utility consumption due to its low operating pressure. When conducting PSA using CMS as the adsorbent, the pressure of the exhaust stream must be increased to match that of the sink system of a recycle connection. As discussed previously (Section 3.1), the CH4 recovery rate of the cases using zeolite 5A as the adsorbent (odd-numbered cases) is higher than that of the corresponding cases using CMS as the adsorbent (even-numbered cases). Therefore, it is obvious that zeolite 5A is more suitable for three-stage CBM separation systems than CMS in terms of technical performance, specifically, CH4 recovery and energy efficiency.

texts.orig: The final metric of the economic evaluation, the methane production cost (MPC), is shown in Fig. 3. The MPC of Case 5 (0.37 $/kg of CH4) is the lowest, mainly because the contribution of capital cost is the smallest in this case. On the other hand, Case 7, which has the lowest variable operating cost (Fig. 2b), is ranked second in terms of lowest MPC (0.47 $/kg of CH4), even though this value is considerably higher than that for Case 5. Therefore, it is obvious that the capital cost has a stronger effect on the MPC than does the variable operating cost in the CBM separation system. Note that the fixed operating cost, which is a substantial contributor to the MPC, is calculated based on the capital cost.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To examine which process parameters and economic parameters have the strongest effect on the MPC, an economic sensitivity analysis was executed based on Case 5, referred to as the base case , as it gave the most favorable MPC (0.37 $/kg of CH4 or 6.9 $/MMBTU). We assumed ± 20% changes in the values of major process parameters and economic factors. For instance, the costs of the PSA adsorbent attrition material and cooling water were not included because their contribution to the MPC is less than 1%. On the other hand, the cost parameters for membrane replacement and electricity purchase were included in the sensitivity analysis because they account for the majority of the variable operating cost.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The sensitivity analysis results are summarized in Fig. 4. Wenote that the MPC is very sensitive to the total capital cost. We also observe that the interest rate (currently assumed as 8%), which is a crucial parameter for amortizing capital costs, is very important. This finding is attributed to the fact that, as observed in the previous section, the annualized capital cost is the dominant contributor to the MPC, ranging between 36% and 41% of this value. The parameters related to the variable operating cost have relatively little effect on the MPC. Nevertheless, the changes in raw material prices have a stronger impact on the MPC than do changes in electricity cost and the make-up material rate and prices.

texts.orig: 6. Conclusions
texts.text: 6. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The aim of this conceptual design study was to evaluate the economics of hybrid CBM separation systems consisting of PSA and membrane processes. To this end, we preliminarily selected ten CBM separation systems based on a previous feasibility analysis (Ko et al., 2013). We then developed rigorous process simulation models to analyze major technical evaluation metrics, such as CH4 recovery and process energy efficiency. Finally, we compared the economics of the proposedsystemsusingtheMPC,findingthatthemethanesupply pathwayfromCBMiscompetitivewiththosefromtheconventional and other renewable-resource-based pathways.
texts.text: The aim of this conceptual design study was to evaluate the economics of hybrid CBM separation systems consisting of PSA and membrane processes. To this end, we preliminarily selected ten CBM separation systems based on a previous feasibility analysis (Ko et al., 2013). We then developed rigorous process simulation models to analyze major technical evaluation metrics, such as CH4 recovery and process energy efficiency. Finally, we compared the economics of the proposedsystemsusingtheMPC,findingthatthemethanesupply pathwayfromCBMiscompetitivewiththosefromtheconventional and other renewable-resource-based pathways.

texts.orig: · Case 7, which begins with a PSA stage and includes a recycle from the third to second stages, shows the best separation performance, with a CH4 recovery of 99.8%.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Case 7 also shows the highest energy efficiency, meeting the design specifications (97% CH4 purity in the product stream and 90% CH4 recovery), due to the absence of a compression process in the first stage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study provides a baseline study and a suite of tools for analyzing the CH4 recovery, process energy efficiency, and economics of a hybrid CBM separation system and identifies the main cost drivers and technical barriers. Although this study has addressed the technical feasibility and economic viability of a CBM separation system, further improvements are crucial for resolving the practical barriers in the operation of the CBM system: the temporal change of CH4/CO2 ratio of CBM feed gas, spatially intermittent CBM reserves and the market access logistics. In addition, we encourage use of systematic approaches for cost reduction and energy efficiency improvement, such as discovery of new materials and technologies using a multiscale modeling theory, and process integration using an optimization technique.
